title: AI can learn new tasks quickly and generalize beyond examples
description: An AI system can adapt from limited demonstrations and preserve competence when the task, domain, language, or input distribution changes.

subQuestions.title: Can it learn from a few examples?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether the system can acquire a new task format from only a small number of demonstrations.
subQuestions.rationale: Few-shot task acquisition is already strong across many structured transformation, extraction, and drafting tasks.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Few-shot task acquisition
proofItems.shortExplanation: Benchmark batteries show top systems learning new task formats from just a handful of examples across varied domains.

subQuestions.title: Can it transfer to new domains, tasks, and languages?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether competence carries across context shifts without task-specific retraining.
subQuestions.rationale: Transfer is increasingly real, but performance still varies sharply with domain novelty and local jargon.

proofItems.title: Controlled study: Cross-domain and cross-language transfer
proofItems.shortExplanation: Cross-domain evaluations show meaningful transfer, but still large drops when specialized terminology or unfamiliar workflows appear.

subQuestions.title: Can it hold up under out-of-distribution inputs?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether competence survives when the deployment distribution differs from the examples used to set the task up.
subQuestions.rationale: Distribution shift remains one of the clearest persistent weaknesses for otherwise capable models.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Out-of-distribution task retention
proofItems.shortExplanation: Generalization stress tests continue to show steep degradation when task inputs depart from the setup examples used to establish the pattern.

subQuestions.title: Can it infer latent rules from demonstrations?
subQuestions.description: Looks for recovery of hidden structure rather than superficial mimicry of the examples.
subQuestions.rationale: Rule induction is promising on moderate complexity problems, but still degrades on compositional or partially ambiguous hidden rules.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Latent rule inference from sparse demonstrations
proofItems.shortExplanation: Rule-discovery benchmarks show real abstraction ability, but with large failure rates once hidden rules become deeply compositional.

subQuestions.title: Can it form reusable abstractions instead of copying surface patterns?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether the system builds task-level structure that can be reused later in a changed setting.
subQuestions.rationale: There is evidence of abstraction formation, but reuse remains inconsistent once the surface form and task wrapper both change.

proofItems.title: Expert blind review: Reusable abstraction formation
proofItems.source: Expert Review Panel on AI Performance
proofItems.shortExplanation: Expert review of transfer tasks found many models building partial abstractions, but not applying them robustly under larger task shifts.
